223144574.review:
I used to love this game, but that was before the 2.0 update.

The 2.0 update introduced Nvidia's ray tracing into the game and the developer decided not to let people turn it off.

This made the game literally unplayable for anyone who doesn't have a high end modern GeForce or a bleeding edge Radeon card even though the game ran perfectly fine before. This isn't the general hyperbole of "This game's unplayable because it runs under some arbitrary number of frames per second so it's unplayable" that you generally see in reviews or forums.

This is "the game crashes to desktop if your card can't do ray tracing extremely well."

If you force the game into running DirectX 11 so it will even get past the splash screens then you're met with a situation where you can see light sources but nothing is illuminated. The floors are black, the walls are black, the tables and chairs are black, There's no ambient light and no amount of gamma correction will offset this.

There have been multiple threads in the forums complaining about this and asking for a way to switch ray tracing off or even just asking for some direct comment which have been met with silence because they seem to only respond to comments in their discord channel. Which the link to has been broken at least since the release of the 2.0 update if not before.

I hate leaving this as my review because the game before this was excellent. It had fantastic atmosphere, it's take on the ghost hunting tools was interesting, most of the gameplay mechanics were functional (if a bit slow sometimes,) But the decision to, more than two years after release, change the technical requirement specifications to something that requires a much higher specification GPU literally overnight makes me have to give it a "not recommended" unless or until they make ray tracing optional.

Edit: To correct one thing I'd been missing, it turns out there is a functional link to their discord server in one place of the community section. However the one in the discussion boards that is pinned where they literally tell you to go for support or reporting is still broken at last check. This in no way changes the substance of the complaints.

I went into Demonologist expecting a fun horror experience, but I ended up facing a serious issue that completely killed my confidence in the game.

I was playing on normal settings, nothing demanding, and my PC is more than capable of handling games like this. I’m running an RX 7800 XT, an i9 processor, a water cooler, and a well-ventilated setup. Despite that, out of nowhere, the game suddenly caused both CPU and GPU usage to spike massively at the same time.

It was completely unexpected. My water cooler even triggered a red high-temperature warning, which almost never happens, and I had to urgently restart my computer to prevent potential damage. There was no gradual buildup, no warning signs, just a sudden and extreme spike.

This raises serious concerns about the game’s optimization. A game like this should not behave that way on high-end hardware, especially on normal settings. It feels like there’s a major technical issue or poor resource management behind the scenes.

Unfortunately, this experience was enough for me to stop playing. A game can have great ideas and atmosphere, but if it risks your hardware like this, it’s hard to recommend.

I really hope the developers address this soon, because in its current state, the optimization is simply not acceptable.

221985718.review:
The game is great when all the mechanics want to work right. The atmosphere is creepy, the locations are all varied and interesting, and the tools are all unique plays on classic ghost hunting items. The animations of the characters are, for the most part, pretty good too. There’s some character customization, base upgrades, and decorations. The jump scares and events are varied and can be terrifying or interesting. There are even things in the environment that you can read aloud which will cause a little surprise which is pretty cool the first few times.

Now for the bad. The basic tools are all mostly good enough to figure out what kind of ghost you’re dealing with. The extras will help you complete the “optional objectives” which in turn will let you exorcise the ghost/demon. You most likely won’t be able to do that without buying some sort of extra tool like a candle or camera. But just one of those tools cost thousands and will require a little grinding/farming. But when you die and lose your extra tools, it really sets the game back due to just how expensive everything is; it feels extremely punishing when you die, and you will die especially while trying to learn the later exorcisms.

Some of the base items don’t seem to be very intuitive or seem to take forever to use. Fingerprints aren’t consistently seen with UV light. The ESG tool (DOTS) and the Easel (Ghost drawing/writing) seemingly take forever to work with no rhyme or reason as to when or why; some games have lasted 30+ minutes of just staring at these last two items hoping for an interaction for a final piece of ghost evidence. Your basic EMF tool is based upon distance of the item interacted with, but doesn't take into consideration if the item is thrown out of reach or in an entirely different room above or below you.

Now there is a bare bones written “how to play” tutorial that you can read, but it does little to really show you how to use each tool and when and why they’ll work.

There are 3 “cursed objects” in the game, but they feel like blatant and cheaper rip offs from another popular ghost hunting game. A ouija board that can only be asked 4 very specific questions with only one being useful. A voodoo doll that can either cause more activity or a hunt, and a tarot deck with cards that cause random effects.

There are 24 ghosts with different personalities, but they’re not very distinct and sometimes don’t even feel like they’re working right; there’s a ghost that angers when it hears cussing and swearing, but a hilarious game was spent doing that without any repercussions. One of the ghosts even has one of their attributes as "we have no data" as if the devs just gave up on adding anything.

Also you can’t complete your optional objects (like take a photo of the ghost or collect “item”) at the same time and while you’re looking for the ghost room and trying to figure out the ghost type. It seems like this is in place just to make you spend more time in the location instead of respecting the player’s time.

Difficulty wise, the game seems pretty balanced and almost too easy at times on “easy”, but if you bump up the difficulty, the ghost seems to interact less and less with its environment meaning you can’t get the evidence you need to ID it. It turns into a game of looking for a possible ghost room that hasn’t had anything happen in it, in the dark (since the lights will supposedly break more and more as you heighten the difficulty, even though some easy games, I’ve had literally every light break), and just walking around until you’re insane enough that the ghost will constantly hunt you. There is a custom mode, but the options are extremely limited and don’t seem very worth it since they’ll tank your money/reward multiplier. 

Each location has a very specific way to exorcise the ghost that won’t change and is always your last objective. All of these also involve running around the map, looking for some sort of object(s) that only spawn in once your optional objectives are done. Some of these, especially on earlier maps, require you to pick up “x” amount of an object (rats, fingers, etc.) and take them somewhere. This is made more frustrating because they count as a tool and you’ll only be able to carry up to 3 at most; they later change this in the later maps thankfully and let you pick up and hold all items at once. Currently, one of the more frustrating exorcisms involves finding an item that will only spawn in the map once you lock in your ghost type (right or wrong), which can spawn anywhere in the house, and also immediately starts a timer for another final part of the exorcism which involves you examining multiples bodies using vague instructions.

Some random low points. There’s no global talk (like a radio system), so don’t think of getting too far away from your friends. Your keybinds reset each and every time you quit the game. The ghosts don’t make noise when they’re hunting you, so it’s hard to pinpoint where they’re at or if they’re done. While you can use your flashlight at the same time as another tool, when you switch to that item to do so, your light turns off and you have to toggle it back on. The jump scares can get repetitive after a few hours (especially after grinding on a map for money) and have nothing to do with a particular ghost type or help in identifying it.

I’ll keep playing this game just to experience each map and unique exorcism puzzle, but afterwards, there’s no way I’m working my way up buying the penthouse base for $150,000. I really want to like this game more than I do, but the core mechanics just don’t feel consistent and the amount of time the game makes you waste in just constantly running around the map feels like it just doesn’t respect your time.

This game feels like it should still be in early access. Wait for a sale if you’re interested after trying the demo and have at least another person to play with. It feels like dev support had dropped off since they called it good and suddenly went out of early access.

For a game that leans so heavily on visuals to sell its horror, the presentation just isn’t there. Until the devs come out with another  graphics overhaul it’s hard to recommend. Unlike Phasmophobia this game relies heavy on jump scares so it's best experienced as a new player & becomes less frightening the more experienced you are.

The player base is small, unless you have friends to play with expect to play solo often for many multiplayer lobbies people set up are locked & blocked unless you have the passcode to play in their lobby. Everytime I get on there were very few joinable lobbies.

221281049.review:
Phasmo feels like a puzzle game with a basic checklist to accomplish your goal. You need to make repeated trips and dump off equipment, occasionally checking them to see if they show anything useful until you finally get enough and you can just waltz out. Once in a while you'll get spooked or get hunted, but with enough time, it just becomes a pretty chill/boring routine.

Demonlogist, from my brief time played, feels like Phasmo, but the theme park version of it. There are many scripted events or "theme park rides" to experience. While Phasmo has some, like a rocking chair moving or items being thrown, the ones used in this game feel more over the top. These are basic run of the mill jump scares. Nothing feels organic or realistic, only very hollywood-escue. I certainly prefer the more subtle and nuanced kinds of spooks and scares versus the "forced" and sudden ones. While there are a nice number of both cool and creative uses of spooks, there are also many basic and uncreative ones. Despite only playing less than two hours, I was able to see the same "spooky" events nearly every single run, completely ruining any chance of the individual run feeling unique.

The equipment differences from Phasmo are nice, in general. I like them and how they did something different that feels natural. However, the complete lack of tutorial and the weak in-game guide and overview of equipment left me perplexed and lost on my first couple runs.

Despite the flaws of Phasmo, feeling incomplete, janky and lazy character and ghost models; it still feels like a far far better game than Demonologist. If Demonologist were to make the game feel less like a clockwork theme park ride and more like an organic ghost hunt, I think this could be something special. I don't see this ever happening. I genuinely feel like this is a cheap knock-off of an already cheap looking/feeling game.

Hard pass.

218632701.review:
As much as I love this game, it has many, many flaws...

I purchased this game shortly after it came out and loved the atmosphere. The base game is great but there are LOTS of bugs to encounter. First off, graphics settings reset quite often, same with your controls. Having to change your settings upon each launch has gotten quite annoying and tedious. Despite the updates they have made addressing this problem, it hasn't changed.

When it comes to the gameplay, like most ghost hunting games, you just kind of wait around until your tools go off. I wish this game had a bit more to do or extra ways to identify the ghost in a more interactive way rather than sitting around in the correct room. After you play for a while you start to quickly understand how this game was developed and you can very easily cheese the mechanics in your favor. For example, the ghost listens to your microphone through the in-game chat system. Once you know the correct keywords to activate the ghost, identifying becomes quite easy and not challenging. You don't even need to ask its name or where it is. The game just hopes the user will YAP into their spirit box until they get scared and get hunted. The VERY scripted jump-scares also become quite laughable after the second time you encounter them. They essentially trigger every time you walk through a doorway or look in the direction of a place that looks like it would have a jump-scare. They are not rare whatsoever which makes them more annoying than scary.

In terms of progression, there really is none. The different safehouses + furnishings you can buy achieve nothing and to be honest, with each new one, they get worse and worse but the developers just make them cost more. There are no activities in the safehouse, you're essentially just customizing your lobby before the match (kinda lame to be honest).

The character customization is also EXTREMELY limited. Barely any clothing to pick from and no unlockable outfits or skins based on level/progression.

I still have high hopes for this game as I think there is a lot of potential. I just think the developers need to really crack down and focus on this game.

The only reason I am currently playing is to farm a lot of money so that when new content DOES come out, although rarely, I can purchase it and play it.

My suggestions; Add daily, weekly, and monthly challenges that reward 'X' amount of money and XP. Please do NOT make them "Complete 'X' amount of matches on (INSERT MAP NAME)". Come up with creative challenges like completing a game in under 'X' amount of time or "correctly identify the ghost in (INSERT TIME FRAME). The steam store page advertises unique challenges but there are absolutely none, false advertising...

I also would like to see a progression overhaul, make customization options players would actually enjoy using such as unique clothing tied to your level, a prestige system which can unlock new cosmetics, trophies for your safehouses, skins for the various tools used to identify the ghosts, etc.

Speaking of customization, the safehouses need a SERIOUS overhaul. With the most recent update, the facility costs the player 150,000 dollars and it is arguably the worst safehouse in the game, the scenery is mostly spread out and empty space, you can't even buy the radio or have pets there. Safehouses should have trophy cases with player achievements in them to show the time and effort players put into this game.

Safehouses should also include activities for the players to participate in, maybe a pool table, a playable arcade machine, etc. Players should have the ability to "hang-out" in this game prior to playing, rather than sitting in a fancy loading screen.

Overall, I hope this game gets the love it deserves. It has a HIGH amount of potential and I would love to see this game succeed. As of now, other ghost hunting games are superior because there is more content within them and progression has a bit more meaning.

Right now, I would say that a player could comfortably get about 50 hours of unique gameplay in this game before it gets too stale. I cannot recommend it at this moment :(

217032219.review:
Let's start with a score. 9/10

This takes the premise established by Phasmophobia and expands on some things I like, but some great opportunities lie with the future of the game. Overall a great game, and I would recommend it. I think just like Phasmo it's best played with friends. I will likely never play this game by myself due to the sheer terror.

This is definitely in the "psychological horror game" more than the "ghost hunting puzzle game" category. The focus is definitely more on the horror and fear of being in a haunted home with unique events for almost every room. Replaying the same homes/maps feels terrifying every time. There isn't an 'endless bucket' of secrets and scary moments so inevitably you will have 'seen it all' but it really will take hundreds of hours before then. And because there's no way to track it all you may still find yourself seeing new events a time or two. With the addition of new haunted locations as well as a map selector that definitely has "blanks" on it that could be used in the future I see this having a great future.

Equipment. There are significantly less tools and variations than their big competitor, but the devil is in the details because I even feel like "All T3 equipment" in Phasmo can be a bit of a drag. Where's the fear when you are able to see the house clearly with a big bright flashlight? Not being able to firmly track the ghost, needing to be in the house more/longer in order to really get a sense of where they are and what they're doing makes it infinitely more interesting and engaging. Couple this with the uniqueness of the haunted locations and this makes for a very engaging and dynamic experience.

Ghost selection. There's really no easy way to put it, it's a little difficult to get used to the ghosts in this game when you have played phasmo so much. There's a lot of reused ghost archetypes between the two games, which is completely fair because they are all(?) found in common mythos. But, it can be a little frustrating when you see "Raiju" and expect a ghost that hates electrical parts from phasmo, and that's just not how it works in this game. It's fun to learn a new list of ghosts and what they do, but it makes me wonder which of the two games are closer to correct with their behavior programming.

Exorcism. I think this is where the game shines. The ability to banish the ghost from the house is what makes you *want* to go back in the house. Knowing that you only need 1 more scroll, or 1 more rat sacrifice before you can leave confidently knowing you completed all available objectives makes you want to go back in- even when that ghost can sniff you from the doorway and will hunt the second you step back inside as you've slipped into insanity.

Constructive Feedback. There's definitely more to be done from a development standpoint. I would love to see tons of new features added to the game. There feels like their focus has been on ambiance, world building, story writing and "how the game works" at it's core being VERY VERY developed and impressive. However, the "list of features" comes short when you compare directly to Phasmo. 
-I'd like to see more equipment. I don't need night vision goggles and a ghost-hunting shotgun but it'd be nice to see more things that let you interact with the ghost, even if it doesn't lead to 'evidence.' Things that maybe let you figure out what the ghost is or maybe just world stuff (ouija board responses, etc).
-A big complaint I always had with Phasmo is the best information was found on google, not in the journal, and I feel we're in the same boat here. Maybe just add a computer in the tent or something, to give you "more information" about the ghosts, something along those lines. There's fear in a lack of knowledge but it takes you away from the immersion anyway if you just alt+tab and hit google.
-Economy feels a little janky right now? I understand "risk/reward" dynamics means you have to be really careful about dying (let's be honest, the fear of death in games isn't high enough, you have to punish players for mistakes somehow) but it feels like you have to do 5 clean runs before you have enough money to buy all the equipment for 1 stacked run. Feels a little off somehow, and maybe I'm just too new to be giving feedback on economy? I feel like the numbers ain't numbering though. Luckily you (mostly) don't need all of the equipment on all maps, but a lack of in-game information means you could waste money on a sledgehammer on the abandoned house.

All in all I will say the game is great, if you like phasmophobia but maybe the scare has kinda been lost on you, be prepared to change your pants. I don't really play spooky games much but this one has gotten some pretty hilarious scaredy responses from me and the people I play with. This game is worth the money, easily, and even when I inevitably drift from the game in the future, this will never be uninstalled as I expect the future for the game to just keep expanding.

215735830.review:
Demonologist is a well-designed and executed game. Among ghost-hunting games, it clearly has the best graphics and jump scares.

To clearly identify the ghosts, we need to determine three of their characteristics, for which we have a number of tools at our disposal, the range of which can be expanded later with the help of the points we earn. The merit of the game is that the goal is not only to identify the ghosts, but also to successfully perform the exorcism, which involves several, mostly interesting tasks. 

However, each ghost behaves differently, and if the player is experienced enough, they can almost immediately determine the type of ghost and immediately proceed to perform the exorcism task. This also demonstrates the sophistication of the gameplay.

It is also important to note that the game offers a completely different experience when played alone or in multiplayer mode. Alone, especially during the first playthrough, it provides a terrifying experience. This is contributed to by both the frightening atmosphere and the jump scares. When playing with others, you can do crazy things or complete tasks quickly to score points faster.

The ability to purchase and customize characters and lobby waiting rooms only adds to the experience, but the developers have not gone overboard.

The maps are unique and well designed. The first few were created to be played through alone, so that players can learn the mechanics, and later on, larger ones appear, which are obviously easier to tackle with more players. There are a decent number of them available, but there aren't enough, and no new ones are being released at the moment, which is the main problem.

Overall, Demonologist is one of, if not the best ghost-hunting/exorcism game out there, and I definitely recommend it.

209352490.review:
The night smelled like smoke and rain—your favorite kind of hunt.

You moved through the ruined chapel with your blade drawn, its edge humming faintly with the charms you’d carved into it at dawn. Shadows pooled like ink along the broken stone floor, but you felt the presence before you saw it: a pulse in the air, warm and electric, brushing against your skin like a whisper of breath.

“Still chasing me?” a voice murmured from the dark.

You swallowed. You knew that voice.
You’d been hunting this demon for months.

And you could never quite decide whether capturing them was your duty…
or your downfall.

They stepped into the faint lantern-light, tall and impossibly calm, ember-gold eyes locked on yours. Not threatening—inviting. Teasing. As if they knew their mere presence caused your pulse to skip.

“You’re late,” they said.

“I wasn’t planning on meeting you,” you replied, though your voice softened despite yourself.

A smirk curved their mouth, slow and wickedly beautiful.

“You always plan on meeting me.”

They circled you—not predatory, but close enough that the warmth of them pushed against your back, your shoulder, your throat. Their energy was intoxicating, a mix of danger and… something strangely gentle.

“Your heart’s racing,” they whispered.

You lifted your chin. “Maybe I’m just excited to win this time.”

A low laugh shivered through the air. You felt rather than saw their hand hover near your waist—never touching, just close enough that your breath caught. The tension between you two had always been its own kind of magic.

“Careful,” they breathed. “If you get too close, you might not want the fight to end.”

Your blade lowered—just an inch.
Their eyes softened—just a fraction.

For a moment it was only you, them, and the quiet promise hanging in the air: a promise of something dangerous, yes… but also strangely sweet, the kind of connection neither of you had words for yet.

“Stay,” they said quietly.

You hesitated… then stepped closer.

Not to surrender.
Not to capture.

But to finally admit that demon or not, this magnetic pull between you was real—and maybe tonight, for just one heartbeat, the hunt could wait.

The lantern flickered.

Their fingers brushed yours—gentle, warm, deliberate.

And everything inside you answered.

Great visuals, effective scares, and solid core gameplay!

Demonologist is a fantastic horror experience that I highly recommend, especially for fans of the co-op ghost-hunting genre.

What I love:

Graphics and Atmosphere: The visuals are truly impressive and do an excellent job of setting a dark and terrifying atmosphere. The maps are also very well designed and look beautiful (in a spooky way!).

Jumpscares: The scares are effective and well-executed, definitely keeping you on your toes!

Equipment and Mechanics: The different ghost-hunting devices and general mechanics are really well made and enjoyable to use, forming a solid foundation for the gameplay loop.

Room for Improvement (Minor feedback):

I would love to see more variety in the equipment/evidence that can be found.

A little more variety in the exorcism processes would also be a nice addition for more diverse rounds.

Overall, the game is top-notch and a very well-made horror title. If you're looking for an atmospheric, scary, and fun co-op horror game, give Demonologist a shot!

205153286.review: As much as I wanted to like this game, I just can’t recommend it. The developers completely abandoned it in favor of Night Club Simulator, and it’s honestly sad to see. This could have been a serious competitor to Phasmophobia, but instead it’s been left to collect dust on Steam thanks to some really poor decisions. The core gameplay isn’t bad, but it feels unfinished. The formula is simple: ghost in a house, identify it, done. Exorcisms and hidden secrets across the maps are a nice touch, but beyond that there’s not much to keep you hooked. Everything is scripted - the events always trigger at the same times, so there’s very little randomness. The ghost models look goofy, and after 5-6 runs on the same map, you’ve basically memorized everything, making ghost hunting more of a chore than fun. Rewards are weak, the progression system feels pointless, and there just aren’t enough items to keep things interesting. Overall, the game isn’t worth the price or your time. A huge shame, really - if the devs had kept working on it, this could have been Phasmophobia’s biggest rival. Instead, it’s a case of wasted potential. Don’t buy it.

204471886.review:
The Good:
Demonologist really nails the scary atmosphere, and that’s what keeps me coming back. The environments are eerie, the sound design builds tension, and the jump scares are both terrifying and hilarious when playing with friends. It’s one of those games where you scream, then immediately laugh because everyone else did too. The co-op element makes it especially fun, since the shared fear creates memorable moments every session.

The Not-So-Good:
That said, the biggest drawback is the lack of updates. From what I’ve seen, the game has hardly evolved since launch. There are still a handful of bugs that remain unfixed, which can be frustrating when they interrupt an otherwise immersive experience. It feels like the potential for the game is huge, but it needs more consistent developer support to keep it fresh and polished.

Overall:
Demonologist is definitely worth playing if you love scary co-op horror experiences and want a game that reliably delivers jump scares and laughter. But unless the devs start rolling out more updates and bug fixes, it risks losing long-term replayability.

199543924.review:
Demonologist on Steam offers an intense, atmospheric experience that immerses players in the haunting world of supernatural investigation and ghost hunting. Designed as a cooperative horror game, it emphasizes teamwork, communication, and a keen sense of observation as players take on the roles of paranormal investigators exploring eerie locations filled with dark lore and chilling secrets. From the moment you step into its foreboding environments, the game establishes a tense and immersive atmosphere through detailed environments, haunting sound design, and a carefully crafted narrative that keeps players engaged and on edge. The graphics and visual effects contribute significantly to the sense of dread, with shadowy corridors, flickering lights, and supernatural phenomena that appear unpredictably, heightening the feeling of vulnerability and suspense.

Gameplay revolves around exploring haunted locations, gathering evidence of paranormal activity, and identifying the type of supernatural entity haunting the area. Players are equipped with a variety of tools such as EMF readers, spirit boxes, UV flashlights, and cameras, each serving specific functions to detect and document ghostly phenomena. The game’s mechanics encourage strategic use of these tools, requiring players to coordinate and communicate effectively to avoid danger while uncovering clues. As evidence accumulates, players can analyze their findings to determine the type of entity they are dealing with, which influences their approach to exorcising or escaping from the ghost. The game employs a risk-reward system, where investigating certain phenomena can increase the chance of attracting the ghost’s attention, making each decision a tense balancing act between discovery and danger.

One of the most compelling aspects of Demonologist is its focus on realism and authenticity. The game draws inspiration from classic ghost hunting stories and paranormal research, which is reflected in its attention to detail and careful adherence to investigative procedures. The narrative is woven through environmental storytelling, audio cues, and the evidence collected during gameplay, creating a layered experience that rewards curiosity and careful observation. The multiplayer aspect enhances the overall experience, as working with others is essential for covering more ground, sharing evidence, and surviving encounters with malevolent spirits. Communication is crucial, and the game’s design encourages players to strategize, warn each other of impending danger, and work together to solve the mystery of each haunting.

The sound design and ambient effects are particularly noteworthy, as they play a vital role in building suspense and immersion. The creaking floors, distant whispers, sudden bangs, and ghostly voices contribute to an unsettling atmosphere that keeps players constantly on alert. The visual effects for paranormal phenomena, such as flickering lights or ghostly apparitions, are both subtle and startling, effectively heightening the sense of unpredictability and fear. The game also features a progression system where players can unlock new tools and upgrades, allowing for more advanced investigations and adding a layer of customization to their investigative style. While the game’s horror elements are rooted in a realistic portrayal of ghost hunting, it also embraces supernatural horror tropes, creating a captivating blend of fear and fascination.

Overall, Demonologist stands out as a thoughtfully crafted paranormal horror experience that combines atmospheric storytelling, strategic gameplay, and cooperative multiplayer elements. Its emphasis on investigation and evidence gathering provides a fresh take on the horror genre, engaging players in a compelling and immersive quest to uncover supernatural truths. The game’s balance of realism, tension, and player interaction makes it an excellent choice for fans of horror, mystery, and cooperative gameplay. While it may appeal most to those who enjoy slow-building suspense and strategic exploration, it also delivers enough scares and adrenaline-pumping moments to satisfy even the most seasoned horror enthusiasts. In all, Demonologist offers a hauntingly memorable adventure that captures the thrill and dread of ghost hunting while providing a richly detailed and atmospheric experience.

Rating: 8/10

Fun for a While – But Jump Scares Fall Flat

I enjoyed this game for a while, especially with friends, but over time I started noticing some issues. Even though the game gets regular updates and new maps, the jump scares just don’t hold up. They’re often the same and start to feel stiff and predictable. You might be thinking, “What’s this guy talking about?”  but when I play a horror game, I’m looking for that rush of excitement and fear. After an hour or two, that feeling fades because the scares lose their impact.

That said, the investigation side of the game is really well done. Trying to figure out which type of demon or ghost you're dealing with is genuinely fun. Some ghosts are like the quiet kid in the back corner of a classroom not super aggressive, but they add a layer of challenge that keeps things interesting. It’s those moments of deduction and strategy that really keep the gameplay engaging.

I’d say the game is worth recommending, especially for the price. It’s more than reasonable, and you can definitely enjoy it with friends for a few solid sessions.

Overall: It’s an OK game not perfect, but it has its moments.
Developers: please spice up the scares!

Demonologist – Creepy, Slow-Burn Ghost Hunting

★★★☆☆ (3.5/5)

Demonologist is a chill paranormal investigation game that leans more on atmosphere and tension than jump scares. You spend most of your time gathering evidence, setting up gear, and trying to stay calm while creepy stuff happens around you.

The scares are more subtle and psychological, which works if you’re into that slow-burn horror vibe. It can feel a bit repetitive at times, but the eerie environments and ghost interactions keep things interesting.

Not the most intense horror game, but if you want a spooky, methodical experience with some cool ghost-hunting tools, Demonologist is worth a shot.
